name: John Cleves Symmes
description: John Cleves Symmes Jr. (1780-1829) was an American army officer and lecturer best known for his Hollow Earth theory. He proposed that the Earth was hollow and habitable, containing multiple concentric spheres within, accessible through large openings at both the North and South Poles. Symmes believed these polar openings were vast, perhaps 4,000 miles in diameter, leading to an inner world illuminated by an inner sun. He envisioned this inner Earth as populated and potentially more advanced than the surface world. Symmes actively campaigned to gain support for an expedition to the North Pole to discover one of these openings. His theory, initially presented in 1818, gained some popular attention, though it was largely dismissed by the scientific community of his time. Mainstream science views the Earth as a solid body with a molten core and a layered structure, based on seismic data, gravitational measurements, and geological studies. The existence of polar openings and an inner world contradicts established scientific principles and lacks any credible empirical evidence. Symmes' ideas are considered pseudoscience and a historical curiosity, rather than a scientifically valid theory.
subtitle: Hollow Earth advocate and theorist

claims.claim: The Earth is hollow and habitable.
claims.evidence: Symmes cited observations of migratory bird patterns near the poles, warmer temperatures further north, and the aurora borealis as evidence of an inner sun.
claims.criticism: These observations have alternative, scientifically valid explanations related to weather patterns, magnetic fields, and the Earth's atmosphere. Seismic data and gravitational measurements confirm the Earth's solid structure.

claims.claim: There are large openings at both the North and South Poles leading to the inner Earth.
claims.evidence: Symmes provided no direct evidence of these openings but relied on conjecture and interpretations of anecdotal accounts from explorers.
claims.criticism: Extensive exploration of both polar regions has revealed no such openings. Satellite imagery and mapping technologies provide conclusive evidence against their existence.

claims.claim: The inner Earth is inhabited by advanced civilizations.
claims.evidence: Symmes speculated about the possibility of civilizations existing within the Earth, but offered no concrete evidence.
claims.criticism: There is no scientific evidence to support the existence of inner Earth civilizations. The extreme conditions within the Earth, including high temperatures and pressures, would make life as we know it impossible.

keyFigures.name: John Cleves Symmes Jr.
keyFigures.role: Originator and chief proponent of the Hollow Earth theory

keyFigures.name: Jeremiah N. Reynolds
keyFigures.role: Early supporter and advocate for a polar expedition inspired by Symmes' ideas

mythologyConnections: Connections to underworld myths in various cultures (e.g., Hades, Agartha)
Themes of hidden realms and inner worlds

timeline.event: John Cleves Symmes Jr. is born.
Symmes circulates his 'Circular No. 1' outlining his Hollow Earth theory.
Symmes travels and lectures promoting his theory.

sources.author: Gardner, Martin
sources.work: Fads and Fallacies in the Name of Science
